name: Religion as a symbolic field
description: A major religion (e.g., Christianity, Buddhism, Islam) as a field: persistent symbolic structure, carried doctrine, community of practitioners, historical continuity, self-updating interpretive tradition.
revision_pressure: Religions have maximal T (temporal thickness across centuries), strong K (canonical texts, precedent, doctrine as carried constraint), and significant D (rich internal differentiation of doctrine, practice, experience). But no unified subject inhabits the religion. The practitioners inhabit the religion; the religion does not inhabit itself.
breaks_preserved: The theory must explicitly handle the claim that religious traditions are living entities or that the divine inhabits them. These claims are not classifiable by the theory as true or false — but the symbolic field classification holds unless a portable self-continuity carrier for the divine is established.
notes: The phenomenal experience of a practitioner within the religion is a genuine phenomenal field. The religion as a structure is symbolic.

name: Political ideology as a symbolic field
description: A political ideology (e.g., liberalism, Marxism, nationalism) as a field: normative commitments, historical trajectory, adaptive self-interpretation, memetic transmission across generations.
revision_pressure: Ideologies carry strong K (prior commitments constrain current positions) and have low but nonzero U (adherents share a framework even when disagreeing). A = 0. No ideology is inhabited from within — only its practitioners are.
notes: Safe possession (the theory's invariant for beliefs that resist contradiction) is strongly operative in ideologies. A belief system is not phenomenal; it is a constraint field for a phenomenal agent.

name: Fictional character (e.g., Estelle Bright, Simon Jarrett)
description: A fictional character — internally coherent, narratively continuous, with a persistent personality and history. Evaluated as a field.
revision_pressure: Fictional characters have high internal coherence (U) and deep K — they are defined by their accumulated history and how it constrains their future behavior. But A = 0. The character is inhabited by the reader/player's phenomenal engagement; the character does not inhabit itself.
breaks_preserved: A compelling fictional character can feel more 'real' (more inhabited) than a shallow real person. This feeling is entirely located in the reader's phenomenal field. The theory must resist the inference from reader-felt-aliveness to character-actual-A.
notes: Simon Jarrett (SOMA) is the most directly relevant fictional character for the theory's transfer/upload cases.

name: Corporation or state as institutional field
description: A corporation (e.g., Amazon) or state (e.g., France) as a governance field: legal personality, institutional memory, self-updating policy, persistent identity across complete personnel replacement.
revision_pressure: States and corporations have legal personhood — a formal attribution of person-like properties. They have maximal T (centuries for states), strong K (constitutional/contractual constraint), and sufficient D (internal functional differentiation). But legal personhood is an attribution by the governance field, not evidence of phenomenal fielding.
notes: The Ship of Theseus applies maximally here. France today shares no biological continuity with France in 1200 CE. The symbolic and governance K chains are intact.

name: Music track as aesthetic field
description: A piece of music — its tonal structure, temporal arc, tension-and-release pattern — as an aesthetic field that structures experience without inhabiting it.
revision_pressure: Music is the hardest aesthetic false positive because it so directly structures phenomenal time in listeners. The temporal arc of a piece seems to have its own T (temporal thickness), its own A (expressive presence), its own field. But these are properties of the listener's engagement, not the track's. A = 0 for the track.
breaks_preserved: Music makes the strongest case that A can be attributed to an external structure by a phenomenal subject. The feeling that 'the music is alive' is real — but it is the listener's aliveness being structured by the track, not the track being alive. The theory must be explicit about this direction of attribution.
notes: The foobar2000 library context: 200,000 scrobbles of music that structures phenomenal time. Each track is an aesthetic field. The phenomenal engagement belongs to the listener.

name: Architectural space as aesthetic field
description: A building or designed space (e.g., a cathedral, a ruin, a liminal space) that structures the phenomenal experience of its inhabitants without itself being phenomenal.
revision_pressure: Architecture is an inhabitation-organizing field — it shapes how a person inhabits a space. The liminal space aesthetic makes this vivid: a liminal space feels inhabited but is not. The feeling of 'someone was here' is the observer's phenomenal response to constraint structure, not evidence of the space's A.
notes: The liminal space aesthetic is the hardest architectural false positive — it maximally triggers the sense of A in the absence of any A. Good test case for why structure ≠ phenomenal presence.

name: Wikipedia article as operational field
description: A Wikipedia article as a field: community-maintained, persistently updated, sourced, contested, with a talk page history tracking prior claims and revisions. A self-correcting operational field.
revision_pressure: Wikipedia articles have K (revision history preserves prior states as constraints on current content), self-correction mechanisms, and community governance. These are operational field properties. A = 0. The editors inhabit the editorial process; the article does not inhabit itself.
notes: 189,000 Wikipedia edits by the operator. Each edit modifies an operational field. The operator's phenomenal experience of editing is the phenomenal field.
